One shared station feeding multiple units (also seen on Pentium Pro, where it issues up to five micro-ops per cycle).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Within a single station, instructions issue in FIFO order. Across stations, there is no guarantee — that's what out-of-order execution actually means.
Dispatching an instruction into a reservation station has four prerequisites:

html:ol.html:li: In-order rule: instructions dispatch in program order (they execute out of order, not dispatch).
Execution unit availability: the matching reservation station has room.
Completion buffer availability: the Reorder buffer has room to track the new in-flight instruction.
Register-rename availability: enough rename registers exist to hold the new instruction's destination.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The mechanism by which a modern out-of-order CPU notices that a previously-stalled instruction now has all its operands ready. The issue queue holds the N in-flight instructions whose operands are not yet available; each entry watches a small set of physical register tags (the operands it is waiting for). When a producer finishes, it broadcasts "tag T is ready" to every entry, and each entry compares that tag against its own waiting tags through a content-addressable memory (CAM). Entries whose last operand arrives become eligible to issue this cycle; a separate select stage picks which of the now-ready entries actually fires.

The cost is the reason CPU issue queues are small. Each entry runs a CAM lookup against each broadcast tag, so an N-entry queue with N producers per cycle scales as O(N^2) in comparators, wires, and power. The select stage compounds this — priority encoders across N entries are themselves a quadratic-area structure on the critical timing path. The cumulative wakeup-select latency is what bounds clock frequency once N grows past a few tens.

This is the structural reason CPU out-of-order windows stay in the tens-to-low-hundreds of entries even as transistor budgets grow. Wider doesn't help: timing closure on the CAM degrades faster than the additional ILP repays. GPUs sidestep the whole problem by leaning on Thread-level parallelism — a cheap per-register scoreboard plus a warp scheduler covers stalls without any broadcast/compare structure.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Wakeup-select sits at the boundary between Reservation station (which holds the instruction) and register renaming (which named the tags being broadcast). All three structures together are what people mean by "the out-of-order back-end."

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two design philosophies for an instruction set architecture.

An ISA with many specialized instructions, including ones that combine memory access with arithmetic or operate on complex types (strings, decimal numbers). One instruction often does the work of several. x86 is the canonical example.
An ISA with a small set of simple, uniform instructions; explicit load and store; arithmetic only on registers. Each instruction does little but maps cleanly to hardware. PowerPC, MIPS, ARM are RISC.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. CISC needs a fat Microarchitecture engine to translate its complex instructions into hardware-executable primitives. That's silicon area, power, and design complexity that doesn't directly add throughput.

Solution. Once RISC clearly won as a hardware model, x86 vendors adopted instruction translation: a CISC front-end decodes x86 into RISC-like Micro-operations internally. The chip is RISC on the inside, CISC on the outside.

Remark. The cost of legacy is real. The original Pentium spent roughly 30% of its transistors on legacy support (mostly the microcode ROM). The Pentium Pro spent 40% on backward compatibility. Modern x86 chips have absorbed this overhead because they can — transistor budgets grew faster than the legacy tax.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The hardware-internal instruction stream that an x86 chip actually schedules. Each user-visible x86 instruction is decoded into one or more micro-ops, which are simpler, more uniform, RISC-like primitives that the back-end can execute, reorder, and rename freely.

Many common x86 instructions translate to just one or two micro-ops, so decoders are split into simple and complex variants — the simple decoders are specific to single-micro-op instructions and run in parallel with the complex one.
Optimizations that operate on the micro-op stream:

html:ul.html:li: Micro-op fusion (Pentium M) — two related micro-ops that the back-end tracks together while sharing a slot in the issue queue. A store decomposes into a store-address and store-data pair, which are fused; a read-modify decomposes into a load + ALU op that fuse and execute serially.

Macro fusion (Core 2 Duo) — fuse a pair of x86 instructions (e.g. compare + branch) before decode, so a single micro-op represents both. Increases peak decode bandwidth without growing ROB or RS pressure.
Trace cache (Pentium 4) — cache decoded micro-op sequences for hot code so decoding doesn't have to happen on every iteration.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Micro-ops exist because CISC vs RISC-style ISAs need to be translated to something the hardware can schedule cleanly. Pure RISC chips don't need them.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An L1-level cache of decoded Micro-operations sequences. Instead of caching raw x86 instructions that have to be decoded on every fetch, a trace cache stores micro-op traces ready for the back-end. Introduced in the Pentium 4 to keep its very deep pipeline fed when normal x86 decoding couldn't run fast enough.
The trace cache operates in two modes:

html:ul.html:li: Execution mode: on a trace hit, feed the cached micro-ops directly to the back-end. Decode is bypassed entirely.
Build mode: on a trace miss, fetch the instructions from L2, decode them, and assemble a new trace while feeding it forward.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The trace cache is indexed during Branch prediction, so speculative paths beyond a branch can be stored in the same trace and fed forward without restarting fetch. This lets a single cache line hold six micro-ops that may include a speculated branch target.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Long x86 instructions that decode to hundreds of micro-ops would blow out the trace cache.
Solution. Tag those instructions. When the trace cache encounters a tag, it stores a pointer to the microcode ROM rather than the micro-ops themselves, and the ROM streams them on demand.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The CPU clock is decreasing much faster than main-memory latency, so without a cache hierarchy a fast CPU spends most of its time stalled on memory.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. If you are a really fast factory, the size of the truck doesn't matter — you must still wait the full duration of the first delivery.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Multiple tiers of progressively-larger, progressively-slower storage between the CPU's registers and main memory. Each tier holds the most-recently-used contents of the next slower tier, exploiting Spatial locality and Temporal locality in the workload.
Approximate tiers on a modern x86 chip:

html:ul.html:li: Registers — ns, dozens of bytes per core.
L1 cache — 2–8 ns, 8–128 KB per core. Often split into instruction and data L1s.
Built from static RAM (4–6 transistors per cell), which is fast but area-expensive vs. DRAM's single-transistor cell.
L2 cache — 5–12 ns, 0.5–8 MB. Closes the latency gap between L1 and DRAM.
L3 cache — slower, shared across cores.
Main memory — 10–60 ns, GBs.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. A byte's journey. L1 checked first; on miss, L2; on miss, main memory; on miss in main memory, Page fault to disk.

Remark. L1 is split into instruction and data caches because the locality profiles of code and data are very different — re-running the same code over different data has high temporal locality for code but low for data.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A cache miss happens when the requested data isn't in cache and has to be fetched from a slower tier. Misses fall into four categories — the "four Cs" of cache misses:

html:ol.html:li: Compulsory miss — the data was never in cache. Cold-start misses are unavoidable for a given cache size and working set.
Conflict miss — the data was evicted because of an associativity collision. A different access mapped to the same set kicked it out. Reduced by higher Cache associativity.
Coherence miss — the data was invalidated because another core wrote to it. Specific to shared-memory multicore systems.
Capacity miss — the cache filled up and the data was evicted by Cache replacement policy. The working set doesn't fit.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Distinguishing them matters because they have different mitigations: conflict misses respond to associativity changes, capacity misses respond to size, coherence misses respond to cache-coherence protocol design, and compulsory misses respond to prefetching.

The empirical observation that if a program accesses memory address x, it is likely to access addresses near x soon. Sequential array traversal, struct field access, and instruction fetch all exhibit it.

Caches exploit spatial locality by loading whole cache lines (typically 64 bytes) on every miss, not just the single requested byte. The byte that triggered the load is the critical word; the surrounding bytes come along for free under the assumption you'll want them.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. RAM and caches are organized in cache-line granularity throughout. Bandwidth is sized for whole-line transfers; tags are per-line, not per-byte.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The complement is Temporal locality — repeated access to the same data over time. Temporal locality implies spatial locality (you re-touch nearby bytes when you re-touch the original), but not vice versa.
If a program accesses memory address x, it is likely to access x again soon. Loop variables, frequently-called functions, and re-used data structures all exhibit it.

Caches exploit temporal locality by retaining recently-accessed lines until something more important takes their slot. The Cache replacement policy is what decides "more important" — typically LRU and the clock algorithm-ish.
Temporal locality implies spatial locality (re-touching a byte tends to re-touch its neighbors) but not vice versa: a sequential scan has spatial locality with no temporal locality.
The rule that decides which slots in the cache an address is allowed to occupy. Three points on the spectrum:

html:ul.html:li: Direct mapped — every address maps to exactly one slot. Fast (only one tag to check) but suffers conflict misses: two hot addresses that map to the same slot evict each other.
Fully associative — any address can live in any slot. No conflict misses but tag check is expensive (compare against every entry in parallel).

n-way set associative — middle ground. The cache is divided into sets of n slots; an address maps to a specific set but can occupy any of its n slots. The hot setting on real caches is somewhere between 4-way and 16-way.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Higher associativity reduces conflict misses but increases tag-check hardware and power. The cache tag of an address is the bits that aren't used to pick the set — they're stored alongside the data and compared on every access.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The tag array is stored separately from the data array (tag RAM) so the tag comparison can happen in parallel with the data lookup. Tags are small, allowing many in-flight comparisons.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Within a Cache associativity set, when a new line arrives and the set is full, which existing line gets evicted? Same question as LRU and the clock algorithm for pages, different timescales.

The hot policy is least recently used — but pure LRU requires tracking a per-line ordering and updating it on every hit, which is too expensive at L1 latencies. Real caches use pseudo-LRU — a tree of single bits per set that approximates LRU with a fraction of the bookkeeping. The clock algorithm is one well-known pseudo-LRU scheme.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Block (cache line) size is part of the same tradeoff. Larger blocks improve Spatial locality but cause cache pollution (you fetch bytes you don't use) and eat memory bandwidth. The sweet spot is around 64 bytes on modern x86.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When a cached line is modified, when do you propagate the change to the next tier?

html:ul.html:li: Write-through — every store updates both the cache and main memory. Simple, but generates memory traffic on every write.

Write-back — stores update only the cache. The line is marked dirty via a per-line bit; the next-tier copy is updated only when the line is evicted. Less memory traffic but the cache is now the sole authoritative source until eviction, which complicates correctness.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Real CPUs run write-back at L1 and L2. Write-through is occasionally used for specific lines (e.g. memory-mapped device registers where every write must hit the device immediately).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Write-back is one of the constraints behind cache-coherence protocols on multicore chips: every core must agree on which one currently owns a dirty line.
The hard limit on per-core clock speed that emerged in the early 2000s, ending the era of frequency scaling. Power dissipation has two components on a chip:

html:ul.html:li: Dynamic power density — watts per unit area dissipated by transistor switching. Scales with clock frequency: faster switching, more energy per second. You can also drive it up by cramming more transistors into the same area.
Static power density — watts per unit area dissipated by leakage current through "off" transistors. Smaller transistors leak more, so denser chips leak more even when idle.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: By the mid-2000s, single-core frequency scaling was thermally unsustainable. The industry pivoted to low-power design and Multicore — get more throughput from many slower cores instead of one faster one. Pentium M and the Core series were the first generations explicitly designed under this constraint.

Multiple independent CPU cores on the same chip. A core, for this purpose, is the bundle of front-end, back-end, Execution unitss, and L1 Cache hierarchy that runs one architectural thread of execution.

Traditional multiprocessor systems put each core on its own physical chip; chip multiprocessing (CMP) puts multiple cores on the same die. The key advantage is shared lower-level caches — typically an L2 or L3 shared across all cores on the chip — which speeds up inter-core communication and amortizes silicon.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The shift to multicore was forced by the Power wall more than chosen for its own merits. The software cost is real: shared-memory parallelism requires correctness work that single-threaded code didn't.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Multicore is what makes Concurrency a hardware problem, not just a scheduling problem: real simultaneous execution on different physical cores means software has to handle Race conditions with real synchronization primitives like Locks.

What the CPU does, entirely in hardware, the moment an interrupt arrives — whether a device IRQ, a fault like a Page fault, or a software int N. The point of the hardware path is to land in the kernel at a clean instruction boundary with enough saved state that user execution can resume later as if nothing happened.
Four things happen, in order, with no software involvement:

html:ol.html:li: Reach a clean instruction boundary. The CPU cannot pause halfway through, say, a memory write — architectural state would be inconsistent. So the current instruction either finishes normally (typical for asynchronous interrupts like a timer) or is rolled back as if it never started (faults, where the instruction couldn't complete anyway and needs to be retried). A page-fault handler maps in the missing page and UHNL returns to re-execute the faulting instruction; a timer handler returns to the next instruction.

Save the minimum resume state to the kernel stack. The CPU pushes RIP (where to resume), RSP (the user stack pointer it's about to abandon), the privilege level (CS — were we in ring 3 or ring 0?), and RFLAGS. The save target is the kernel stack, whose address the CPU reads from the Task state segment that the OS set up at boot. This is a security boundary: the kernel never trusts the user stack, since user code could have corrupted it or pointed RSP at something malicious. Note what is not saved: general-purpose registers. The handler itself pushes RAX, RBX, ... if it uses them, which keeps the hardware path fast.

Jump to the handler via the Interrupt descriptor table. The CPU takes the interrupt number N, indexes entry N of the IDT (found via the IDTR register), loads the handler address into RIP, and loads the new code segment, which switches the CPU from ring 3 to ring 0. After this step, the CPU is running kernel code on the kernel stack at the right privilege level — all set up automatically.

Flush the Reorder buffer of work past the interrupt point. In an out-of-order execution CPU the ROB may already contain instructions from after the interrupt — they've been issued speculatively because the front-end ran ahead. Those entries are discarded so the handler sees clean architectural state, as if execution had stopped exactly at the interrupt point. When UHNL returns, those instructions re-execute normally. This is the same squash machinery used for branch mispredictions in Speculative execution — and, famously, the reason Spectre-class side channels exist: speculative work leaves observable traces in caches even after the ROB is flushed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Once the hardware path finishes, the OS's interrupt handler is running. Its first job is typically to save the remaining registers before doing real work — the rest is software.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Steps 2 and 3 are why an OS needs to set up the IDT and TSS before enabling interrupts. Until both structures point somewhere valid, the first interrupt would either jump to garbage or push to a user-controlled stack — both fatal.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The CPU's lookup table from interrupt number to handler. The OS allocates the IDT in memory, fills entry N with the address of the handler for interrupt N, and points the IDTR register at the table's base. From then on the Hardware interrupt path uses the IDT to dispatch every interrupt without any further software help.

Each entry — a gate descriptor on x86 — carries enough information for the CPU to switch into the handler safely: the handler's linear address, the code segment to load (which sets the new privilege level, typically ring 0), and a type byte distinguishing interrupt gates (which mask further interrupts on entry) from trap gates (which don't). Entries 0–31 are reserved for CPU-defined exceptions (divide-by-zero, Page fault, double fault, ...); 32–255 are available for device IRQs and software interrupts.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The IDT is one of three OS-installed tables that the CPU consults transparently — the others are the GDT (segment descriptors) and the page tables. Each is a hardware contract: the OS lays bytes out in a format the silicon understands, then hands over a pointer.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A small OS-allocated structure that the CPU reads to find the kernel stack pointer when it raises privilege. Its historical purpose — full task switching in hardware — is dead on modern x86-64; what survives is one essential field: RSP0, the address of the kernel stack to switch to on entry from ring 3.

During the Hardware interrupt path, the moment the CPU decides to push RIP/RSP/CS/RFLAGS, it reads RSP0 from the current TSS (located via the GDT entry pointed at by the TR register) and switches RSP to that value before pushing. If the TSS were missing or pointed at user memory, the very first interrupt would either fault while trying to push, or push the user's registers onto attacker-controlled memory. So the TSS is set up at boot, before interrupts are enabled.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. This is why a kernel needs a per-CPU TSS, not a per-process one: RSP0 is consulted on every transition into the kernel, and each CPU needs its own kernel stack to land on.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Machine-instance notes — concepts these chips introduced live in their own atomic notes; this tree records what was novel about each implementation.
Concepts in play:

html:ul.html:li: Cache hierarchy — Pentium adopted L1/L2 caching and split the L1 into separate instruction and data caches.
Branch prediction — branch history table (BHT) and branch target buffer (BTB) combined with static prediction as a fallback on BHT miss.
Speculative execution — execute past a predicted branch, write back only after the prediction is confirmed.

html:ol.html:li: Fetch / Prefetch: read from instruction cache.
Decode 1: decode to internal format; branch prediction.
Decode 2: convert complex instructions via Microarchitecture to Micro-operations.
Execute.
Write.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Back-end: two integer ALUs (also doing address calculations) and one floating-point ALU. The FP register file was arranged as a stack, with most FP ops operating on the top — zero-cycle stack swaps existed but still occupied a decode slot.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Estimated 30% of Pentium transistors went to legacy support, mostly the microcode ROM.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Concepts introduced:

html:ul.html:li: out-of-order execution — instructions execute in dataflow order, commit in program order.
Reorder buffer — 40-entry ROB tracks all in-flight instructions and assists register renaming.
Reservation station — three-instruction dispatch, up to five-issue per cycle.
CISC vs RISC decoupling — x86 in front, Micro-operations internally.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Simple and complex decoders are split because many CISC instructions translate to one or two micro-ops — the simple decoders handle those in parallel with the slow complex decoder.
Remark. 40% of Pentium Pro transistors went to backward-compatibility support.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Machine-instance notes for the PowerPC line — a RISC implementation of the same concepts covered in superscalar and Branch prediction, with a few innovations specific to this family.
Front-end. standard 4-stage pipeline. Instruction queue dispatches 3 instructions per cycle.

html:ul.html:li: Identifies unconditional branches (and resolved conditional ones) in the queue; if taken, deletes the branch instruction and starts fetching from the target — branch folding.
Untaken branches "fall out" — fall through.
No Reorder buffer-equivalent. Static scheduling.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Back-end. one integer ALU (also handles addresses), one floating-point ALU with single-cycle fused multiply-add. For FP-heavy code the integer ALU just does addresses.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Competed with two-integer-ALU designs because simpler logic left room for 4× the L1 cache (32KB vs 8KB).

html:ul.html:li: Branch dispatch on a separate bus, so branches don't compete with non-branch dispatch.
Up to 2 non-branch instructions dispatched per cycle.
Adds a commit unit and completion queue — the PowerPC names for Reorder buffer. Dynamic scheduling with an instruction window.
Two added execution units: a system unit for misc integer ops and a load/store unit. Lighter load on the integer ALU.
FPU took hits: 3 instructions every 4 cycles (mandatory bubble); double precision (including fmadd) takes 2 cycles in execute.
Splits decode/dispatch and writeback into two stages — enables shorter clock cycles.
Up to 6 instructions dispatched per cycle.
Dynamic Branch prediction with a 512-entry BHT (2 bits per entry) and a 64-entry branch address cache (BTB).
Adds a condition-register unit sharing dispatch bus with the branch unit.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Back-end. two simple integer units + one complex; fully pipelined FPU.
Dispatch obeys Reservation station rules: in-order dispatch, execution-unit availability, ROB availability, and rename-register availability.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Shallow pipeline → small ROB and few reservation stations are acceptable. Tradeoff: lower clock speed.
Remark. The 604e doubled L1 to 64KB and added a condition-register unit, freeing the integer and branch units from condition computations.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Same 4-stage pipeline as the 603e. Two integer units instead of one; 3-cycle single-precision FP; 6-entry instruction queue, up to 4 fetched per cycle.

The 750's signature trick: a branch target instruction cache (BTIC). A traditional BTB stores the address of the branch target — you still have to re-fetch that instruction. The BTIC caches the actual first instructions at the target, eliminating the re-fetch latency. This makes Branch prediction dramatically more useful.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Did not support a SIMD instruction set.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Added a vector permute unit (VPU) and vector ALU (VALU) for SIMD; full double-precision FPU; 8-entry instruction queue.
Two contrasting approaches to using a transistor budget, well-paired as instance studies because they target the same constraints from opposite directions.
Optimizes for instruction-level parallelism. Seven-stage pipeline.

html:ol.html:li: Additional second fetching stage.

Issue stage. To avoid in-order issue stalls, the G4e uses issue queues (general / vector / float) that issue 3, 2, and 1 instruction per cycle respectively. Issue queues differ from Reservation stations in that each queue feeds a group of execution units, not a single one.
Complete stage.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Optimizes for clock speed. 20-stage pipeline — extreme depth for fast clock, with the obvious downside that mispredicted branches are very expensive.

html:ul.html:li: Needs deep buffers (126-entry Reorder buffer) to keep the fast back-end fed.
x86 decoding was too slow to keep a 20-stage pipeline fed.
The trace cache moves to L1, storing already-decoded Micro-operations traces; the raw instruction cache moves to L2.
Trace cache is indexed during Branch prediction, so speculative paths past a branch can be stored in the same trace.
Operates in two modes — execution mode (trace hit, feed micro-ops directly) and build mode (trace miss, decode and assemble while feeding forward).

fr:title.text: Pentium M, Core Duo, Core 2 Duo

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Pentium M, Core Duo, Core 2 Duo

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The post-Pentium-4 generations, designed under the Power wall. Each generation contributed an innovation to the concept stack.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A processor often has three names: a brand name, a microarchitecture brand name, and a microarchitecture code name.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: First chip explicitly designed for low power. Innovations:

html:ul.html:li: Widened fetch buffer to 64 bytes — decodes up to 4 16-byte x86 instructions at a time.

Micro-op fusion — pairs of Micro-operations from the same x86 instruction (e.g. store-address + store-data, or load + ALU op for a read-modify) are tracked together as one queue entry until issue. See Micro-operations.
Loop detector in Branch prediction — counters watch loop trip counts; loops that iterate the same number of times each invocation can be predicted with 100% accuracy.
Indirect predictor — like a BHT but stores each branch's favorite target address.
Stack execution unit — dedicated unit with its own adder to handle push/pop ESP updates without going through the integer ALU. Stack micro-ops never reach the back-end.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The first true Multicore x86: two cores on the same die, sharing L2. Innovations relative to Pentium M:

html:ul.html:li: Every decoder is capable of micro-op fusion (up to 3 fused entries into the micro-op queue per cycle).
Fuses vector SSE instructions in addition to scalars.
Improved loop detector for short loops.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Until this point, the "core" was an abstraction — front-end + back-end + L1. The Core Duo is when "core" became a thing chips have more than one of.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Theme: "more is always better" — wider decode/dispatch/issue, bigger Reorder buffer, more Reservation stations, more decode logic. Two innovations made the extra resources pay off:

html:ul.html:li: Macro fusion (front-end) — fuse pairs of x86 instructions (e.g. compare + branch) before decode. Max decode bandwidth grows to 5 x86 instructions per cycle without burning ROB or RS entries.

Memory disambiguation (back-end) — apply Speculative execution to memory aliasing. Loads can speculatively execute ahead of stores with unknown target addresses; on actual alias detected at commit time, the load and dependent ops are squashed and re-executed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The point of memory disambiguation: stores must commit in program order relative to other stores, and no load can commit ahead of an aliased store. But the rule "no load past a store-data with unknown target" forced a lot of unnecessary stalls — most unknowns aren't actually aliases. Speculate and verify.
Back-end also gained single-cycle vector ops: a 128-bit data bus to the vector ALU replaced the previous 80-bit bus, so 128-bit SSE instructions decode as a single micro-op.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A load is one micro-op because address computation and memory access are inherently serial. A store is two micro-ops (store-address + store-data) because address computation and reading the register file can happen in parallel.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: n-bit computing refers to how many bits a register can hold. 64-bit means registers hold 64 bits, 64-bit instructions means their operands are 64 bits wide.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Instruction encoding size hasn't actually changed — the PC widened because the virtual address space did.
Remark. Only integer and addressing units widened. Floating point and vector units already operated on wider registers.

html:ul.html:li: Increased dynamic range — values can range over 2^{64} instead of 2^{32}. Overflow/underflow happens at the extremes of the representable range.
64-bit address space. x86-64 currently implements 40-bit virtual addresses but the architecture allows far more.
Drawback: pointers now take up twice the cache space.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. It is not always beneficial to use 64-bit integers. If you don't need the range, the wider type wastes cache space. The default integer size on x86-64 is still 32 bits; widening requires a one-byte prefix, adding less than 10% to code size.
